coredata.dc:title: Intrinsically ESIPT-exhibiting and enhanced emission in polymer nanoparticles as signaling for sensing nitrite
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Qinge
He, Lingzhi
Zeng, Dan
Zou, Wu
Gong, Fuchu
Xia, Jiaoyun
Cao, Zhong
coredata.dc:description: A straightforward approach to the fabrication of intrinsically excited-state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT)-fluorescent polymer nanoparticles (e-PNPs) was developed. The e-PNPs were obtained by self-assembly of the homopolymers derived from 4-aminosalicylic acid in aqueous solution. By incorporating ESIPT modules into polymer nanoparticles, the ESIPT reaction can be endowed with moderate hydrophobic micro-environment by nanoparticle scaffolds, eliciting enhanced ESIPT emission. The newly developed e-PNPs exhibit strong tautomeric fluorescence(e-FL), good photostability, low-toxicity and favourable biocompatibility in aqueous solution. Upon the addition of NO2 -, the e-FL can be significantly quenched owing to the reaction of NO2 - with the amide groups on e-PNPs. From this basis, the fluorescence detection of NO2 - was implemented, which showed a linear relationship between 0 nM and 110 nM with a detection limit of 2.3 nM. Furthermore, e-PNPs were used as nanoprobes to monitor the NO2 - levels in HeLa cells by fluorescence imaging, demonstrating the ability of discrimination from different concentrations of NO2 -. The proposed method can be applied to a wide range of other ESIPT modules to integrate into polymer nanoparticles and offer highly sensitive nanosensing platform for bioanalysis and molecular imaging.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Polymer nanoparticles
ESIPT-Exhibiting
Tautomeric emission quenching
NO2 - sensing

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing behavior of e-PNPs to nitrite

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (a) and AFM (b) image of e-PNPs; (c) The size distributions of e-PNPs; (d) FT-IR spectrum of e-PNPs.
(a) XPS full scan spectrum of the prepared e-PNPs; (b), (c) and (d) are the C1s, N1s and O1s spectra of the prepared e-PNPs, respectively.

(a) Absorption spectra of e-PNPs and the e-PNPs with the addition NO2 - in a PBS buffer solution (pH7.0), Inset: Digital pictures of e-PNPs and e-PNPs + NO2 - under sunlight; (b)The excitation spectra of e-PNPs in a PBS buffer solution (20 mM, pH7.0); (c) ESIPT emission spectra of e-PNPs in a PBS buffer solution with increasing excitation wavelengths from 320 nm on the shift of 20 nm increments; (d) Fluorescence emission spectra of e-PNPs and e-PNPs with the addition of NO2 - in a PBS buffer solution (pH7.0), Inset: Digital pictures of e-PNPs and e-PNPs + NO2 - solution under UV light illumination. e-PNPs: 0.05 μg/L; NO2 -: 0.02 μM/L.

(a) Selectivity of the e-PNPs nanoprobes to NO2 - over the interferent species; (b) Response profiles of e-PNPs to NO2 - in the presence of different interfering materials. The concentrations: NO2 -, 1.0 μM/L; the others, 2.5 μM/L; e-PNPs concentration was 0.10 mg/L in PBS (20 mM, pH7.0). The incubation temperature was at room temperature, and incubation time was 10 min. The excitation and emission is at 360 and 520 nm, respectively. The slit widths of emission and excitation were 5 nm.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of e-PNPs in the presence of different concentrations NO2 -, including 0, 0.01, 0.03, 0.05, 0.07, 0.09, 0.11 and 3.5 μM (from top to bottom, respectively) with excitation at 360 nm; (b) The linear relationship between F/F
0 and NO2 - concentrations in the range of 0 nM–110 nM.

Confocal microscopic images of HeLa cells incubated with the e-PNPs probes (d) and the HeLa cells with the pretreatment of 1.5 (e) and 10 μM(f) NO2 − before incubation with the nanoprobes, respectively. (a), (b) and (c) are the bright fields of (d), (e) and (f), respectively.
(a) Schematic illustration of the preparation of e-PNPs using 4-aminosalicylic acid as precursors; (b) The sensing mechanism of e-PNPs for nitrite detection.
Analytical results of NO2 - in real samples using e-PNPs.

head.ce:title: Intrinsically ESIPT-exhibiting and enhanced emission in polymer nanoparticles as signaling for sensing nitrite

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Food Engineering, Changsha University of Science and Technology, Changsha, 410114, PR China

Changsha University of Science and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemistry and Food Engineering, Changsha University of Science and Technology, Changsha, 410114, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Food Engineering
Changsha University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A straightforward approach to the fabrication of intrinsically excited-state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT)-fluorescent polymer nanoparticles (e-PNPs) was developed. The e-PNPs were obtained by self-assembly of the homopolymers derived from 4-aminosalicylic acid in aqueous solution. By incorporating ESIPT modules into polymer nanoparticles, the ESIPT reaction can be endowed with moderate hydrophobic micro-environment by nanoparticle scaffolds, eliciting enhanced ESIPT emission. The newly developed e-PNPs exhibit strong tautomeric fluorescence(e-FL), good photostability, low-toxicity and favourable biocompatibility in aqueous solution. Upon the addition of NO2 -, the e-FL can be significantly quenched owing to the reaction of NO2 - with the amide groups on e-PNPs. From this basis, the fluorescence detection of NO2 - was implemented, which showed a linear relationship between 0 nM and 110 nM with a detection limit of 2.3 nM. Furthermore, e-PNPs were used as nanoprobes to monitor the NO2 - levels in HeLa cells by fluorescence imaging, demonstrating the ability of discrimination from different concentrations of NO2 -. The proposed method can be applied to a wide range of other ESIPT modules to integrate into polymer nanoparticles and offer highly sensitive nanosensing platform for bioanalysis and molecular imaging.

Intrinsically ESIPT-fluorescent polymer nanoparticles (e-PNPs) were prepared using 4-aminosalicylic acid. The e-PNPs exhibit strong tautomeric fluorescence, high sensitivity to nitrite, low-toxicity and favourable biocompatibility, making them an outstanding candidate for designing nanoprobes for nitrite detection.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent chemosensors are a class of powerful tools for analytical and biological imaging applications because of their high sensitivity, selectivity, fast response and technical simplicity [1–3]. Generally, a fluorescent sensor consists of a fluorophor core and recognition functional groups, with a built-in transduction mechanism that converts recognition events into the variation of optical performance of the fluorescent moiety [4]. However, conventional small fluorophores often suffer from some inherent limitations, such as photobleaching, limited brightness, poor biocompatibility and low water-solubility. In addition, some of them (e.g., rhodamine, fluorescein and BODIPYs) are usually associated with small Stokes shift, implying that there is a risk of greater self-absorption and suppressed sensitivity.

To surmount the limitations of brightness, biocompatibility and photostability of the small fluorescent dyes and fluorescent proteins, considerable attention have been focused on the nanofluorophores including semiconductor quantum dots (SQDs), carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and polymer assemblies/nanoparticles (PNPs) in the recent years [5–8]. Among these nanofluorophores, soft organic material based PNPs can open the way to a better control of the luminescence brightness, colors, particle diameter, surface chemistry, etc. through increased flexibility by choosing, combining, and organizing their constituents [9–12]. Conjugated polymer nanoparticles (CNPs), where the π-polymers may directly work as the chromophore, have emerged in recent years and produced a number of very exciting examples of FPNPs with exceptional brightness [13–15]. However, similarly to SQDs, the CNPs are intrinsically non-biodegradable polymers and require a specially adapted organic shell to make them stable [16]. So an alternative mainstream strategy is to incorporate various small fluorescent dyes or lanthanide complexes into a non-fluorescent, nanostructured polymer matrix [17]. This approach is highly promising because that it can benefit from taking advantage of biocompatible and biodegradable polymers as matrix materials. On the other hand, such PNPs have demonstrated several advantages over the original fluorophores. Firstly, the integration of fluorescent reagents into PNPs matrices can significantly enhance their water-solubility, while retaining their unique optical properties and endowing them with highly structural stability. Secondly, the hydrophobic micro-environment of polymer scaffolds is very advantageous to improve the fluorescence quantum yield of the embedded dyes, especially for those with weak emission in a polar organic solvent or aqueous solution. Moreover, the polymer matrices can bring the composite systems about additional sites for further functionalization, designing versatility and accompanying with the construction of multifunctional nanomaterials. From these basis, a nanofluorophore by covalently anchoring aminopyrene onto mesoporous silica nanopartilces have developed and used for the determination of nitrite [18]. A NO2 - response probe based on the immobilization of Lauth’s violet on triacetyl cellulose-based nanomembrane has been reported [19]. By immobilizing 2, 3-diaminonaphthalene/cyclodextrin complexes in a porous silicate glass matrix, a nitrite fluorimetric sensor was also constructed [20]. However, these methodologies to the fabrication of FPNPs face the challenge of the leakage of the embedded small fluorescent reagents from nanostructured matrices in practical application.

Excited-state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT) is a phototautomerization occurring in the excited states of some molecules and often resulting in large Stokes shift and dual emission signals, which shows unique advantages over traditional fluorophores and is especially favourable for ratiometric detection in biological systems [21–23]. ESIPT-exhibiting dyes including 2-(2-hydroxyphenyl) benzoxazole, 1-aminoanthraquinone, 2-(2-hydroxyphenyl)-4(1H)-quinazolinone and 3-hydroxyflavone have been used to design attractive signal transducers in sensors [24–26]. Many ESIPT based sensors were constructed for ratiometric sensing of anion, cation, enzyme activity, and signaling molecule and showed unique advantages in sensitivity, selectivity and simplicity over conventional fluorescent dyes. Despite their respective advantages of the reported sensors, similarly to traditional small fluorophores, these small ESIPT dyes face the challenge to break through the limitation of fluorescence brightness, biocompatibility and photostability [27,28]. Another drawback of the ESIPT dyes is that their ESIPT process will be strongly suppressed in water or polar solvents. So it is the key to solve this problem for giving full play to the advantages of ESIPT luminescence.

Nitrite ion (NO2 −) has been widely used as a food additive and preservative agent in food and beverages because that it can suppress the propagation of Clostridium botulinum and improve the flavor of meat. Nevertheless, excessive intake of NO2 − with water or food can be fatal, especially for the pregnant women and infants. NO2 − can facilitate irreversibly react with hemoglobin and inhibit oxygen transportation capability of the blood leading to the generation of deformities and cancer [29,30]. Additionally, in acidic environment, NO2 − acts as reactant for the generation of highly carcinogenic N-nitrosamines [31,32]. Thus, the development of high sensitive detection of NO2 − in water, food, biosample and environment is very important.

In this work, we presented a straightforward approach to the fabrication of intrinsically excited-state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT)-exhibiting polymer nanoparticles (e-PNPs) by reacting 4-aminosalicylic acid in o-dichlorobenzene. The e-PNPs showed strong tautomeric fluorescence (e-FL). Upon the addition of nitrite, the e-FL of e-PNPs can be dramatically quenched. On this basis, a fluorimetric method for the detection of nitrite was then established. A possible mechanism has been suggested where by N-nitroso compounds can be formed by reacting amide group with nitrous acid, which elicits e-FL static quenching. Our experimental results demonstrate that this proposed assay has robustness for the quantitative analysis of nitrite with high sensitivity, low cost and good selectivity.

Unless special stated, all chemicals and other solvents were purchased commercially and used as received without further purification. Double-distilled water was used for the preparation of all solutions. 4-aminosalicylic acid (4-ASA), polyphosphoric acid, 2-hydoxy-N-phenylbenzamide (2-HPA) and sodium nitrite were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Stock 0.1 g/L nitrite solution was prepared in PBS buffer (20 mM, pH7.0). Stock 0.05 g/L solution of e-PNPs was also prepared in PBS buffer (20 mM, pH7.0). Working solutions were prepared by diluting the stock solutions to an appropriate volume with PBS buffer whenever required. The human cervical cancer HeLa cell line was purchased from Shanghai Institutes for Biological Sciences (Shanghai, China).

Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were acquired by a PHILIPS TECNAI 10 TEM instrument (Philips, Netherlands). Atomic Forces microscopic (AFM) images were recorded on MFP-3D Classic AFM (Oxford, United Kingdom). Infrared absorption spectra were recorded with a NICOLET AVATAR 330 Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Nicolet, USA). Fluorescence (FL) spectra were obtained on a F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). UV-Vis absorption spectra were recorded with the UV-Visablem Puxi spectrophotometer (Beijing, China). Fluorescence imaging experiments of HeLa cells were carried out on a confocal laser scanning microscopy (Zeiss LSM 710NLO, Germany). The pH measurements were performed with a pH-meter PB-10 (Sartorius, Germany). The absorbance of the cell viability by CCK-8 was tested using a microplate reader (Thermos Scientiﬁc Multiskan GO, Finland). The absorption, ﬂuorescence spectra and imaging experiments of the resulting solutions were performed at room temperature.

The intrinsically ESIPT-fluorescent polymer nanoparticles (e-PNPs) were prepared by polycondensation and self-assembly. Briefly, 4-aminosalicylic acid (1.53 g, 100 mM) and polyphosphoric acid (0.34 g, 10 mM) were dissolved in 1, 2-dichlorobenzene (20 mL). The mixture was then reaction at 180 for 4 h, and the reaction solution finally turned to dark brown, which suggested the formation of the homopolymers (poly (4-aminosalicylic acid)). After removal of the solvent, 20 mL ultrapure water was added into the residue for ultrasonic dissolution. The resulting supernatant was collected by removing the large precipitates through centrifugation at 10000 rpm for 25 min, and then dialyzed against deionized water through a dialysis membrane (1.0 kD) for 24 h to remove small molecules. Finally, the cinnamon-colored power was obtained after vacuum-freeze drying of the resulting e-PNPs solution.

An appropriate amount of e-PNPs (1.0 mL, 0.25 μg/mL) and nitrite (0.1–2.0 mL, 1–20 μM/mL working solution were transferred into a 25 mL volumetric flask. The mixture was diluted to 10 mL with a PBS buffer (20 mM, pH 7.0) and allowed to stand for 10 min at room temperature. The ESIPT fluorescence (e-FL) intensity was measured at 520 nm excited at 360 nm.

To examine the sensitivity of e-PNPs to NO2 -, 1.0 mL e-PNPs (0.25 μg/mL) aqueous solutions was added into a 9 mL PBS buffer (pH 7.0) containing various concentrations of NO2 - (0, 0.01, 0.03, 0.05, 0.07, 0.09, 0.11 and 3.5 μM). Then, to determine the sensitivity of e-PNPs, we choose a series of ions (Zn2+, Na+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Co2+, NO3 -, SO4 2-, Cl-, ClO-, CO3 2-, Ac-, Br-, H2PO4 -, F-, HCO3 -, P2O7 2-, HSO4 -) and biologically related molecules (H2O2 and ATP) instead of NO2 - to record the e-FL spectra. The concentrations of interference species were 2.5 μM, respectively. The e-FL values were measured for three times. All the e-FL spectra were recorded at 520 nm and excited with a 360 nm laser after reaction for 10 min at room temperature.

Smoked pork and sausage products were purchased from local supermarket in Changsha and triturated. An appropriate amount of each sample (10 g) was weighed and placed in a beaker, then 20 mL Na2B4O7·10H2O solution (5%) and 300 mL hot distilled water were added into the beaker. The mixtures were warmed to 80–90 °C in a hot water bath for 20 min, then cooled to room temperature, and the resulting mixtures transferred to volumetric flasks. 10 mL K4Fe(CN)6 solutions and 10 mL Zn(CH3COO)2 solutions were added to precipitate the protein, and made the volume up to 500 mL, followed by filtration through a filter paper. The filtrate was collected and stored in refrigerator (4 °C) for reserve. Each test time, a 100-μL portion of the filtrate was determined using the procedure described in Section ‘ Procedure for NO
2
-
determination ’.

Pickled vegetables were also obtained from local supermarket (Changsha, China). After cleaning and drying with a blower in room temperature, the pickled vegetable samples were crushed before use. 15.0 g pickled vegetable was weighed and placed in a beaker, then 30 mL Na2B4O7·10H2O solution (5%) and 350 mL of hot distilled water were added into the beaker. The mixture was heated in a hot water bath (80–90 °C) for 15 min, then cooled to room temperature, and transferred into a 500 mL volumetric flask. 15 mL K4Fe(CN)6 solution (10%) and 10 mL Zn(CH3COO)2 solution (10%) were added, and made the volume up to 500 mL, followed by filtration through a filter paper. The filtrate was stored in a refrigerator (4 °C). Each time, a 30-μL portion of the filtrate was determined using the procedure described in Section ‘ Procedure for NO
2
-
determination ’

HeLa cells were used as model cell to test the in vitro cytotoxicity of e-PNPs based on the MTT assay. Firstly, Hela cells were incubated in a 96-well plate (5000 cells/well), supplemented with a 100 μL DMEM containing 10% fetal bovine serum under humidified 5% CO2 atmosphere at 37 °C. After a 24 h incubation, the medium was replaced, followed by adding various concentrations of e-PNPs for a 24 h-incubation. Next, 25 μL MTT reagent solution (10 mg/mL) was added into each well, further incubated at 37 °C for another 4 h. Finally, the medium was removed and 200 μL of DMSO was added and shaken for 10 min in the dark. The absorption of the resulting mixtures was recorded by a microplate reader at the wavelength of 490 nm.

Intracellular distribution of e-PNPs against HeLa cells and the fluorescence imaging of intracellular NO2 - were investigated using CLSM. Typically, HeLa cells were laid flat in a 35 mm glass-bottom culture dish at a density of 1 × 104 cells per dish, letting it grow overnight in a 2 mL DMEM with 10% fetal bovine serum with 5% CO2 humidified atmosphere at 37 °C for 24 h. After that, the original medium was eliminated and a 2 mL fresh medium containing e-PNPs (5 mg/L) was then added into each dish. Followed by incubation for 12 h, ice-cold PBS buffer (20 mM, pH 7.4) was added to rinse the remaining e-PNPs. After the treatment with different concentrations of NO2 − (0, 1.5 and 10 μM) for 1 h-incubation, the cells were incubated in a fresh medium containing e-PNPs (5 mg/L) for 4 h. After rinsing with a ice-cold PBS buffer for three times, the resulting cells then further observed under CLSM.

Synthesis of e-FNPs . In this study, we developed a simple and efficient approach to the fabrication of intrinsically ESIPT-fluorescent polymer nanoparticles (e-PNPs) using 4-aminosalicylic acid as precursors. The synthetic route of e-FNPs is outlined in Scheme 1 a. Firstly, a homopolymer was synthetised by condensation reaction using 4-aminosalicylic acid as monomer and polyphosphate as catalyst. Subsequently, the polymers self-assemble in aqueous solution to form nanostructured e-PNPs. Compared with conventional fabrication of e-PNPs achieved by incorporating small fluorophores into polymer matrices, the present “synthesis-self-assembly” strategy is simpler and more efficient, especially in the preparation of ESIPT-exhibiting polymer nanostructures.

Characterization of e-FNPs
. Following the aforementioned preparation protocol, we have achieved the e-PNPs by condensation and self-assembly. As shown in TEM and AFM images in Fig. 1 , the e-PNPs were well monodispersed and uniform with an average size of 50 nm. To confirm the presence of various functional groups in e-PNPs, the fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra was measured. In the FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1d), the absorption band at about 3379 cm-1 represents the stretching vibrations of O-H, the peak at 2971 cm-1 is attributed to the N-H vibrations. Simultaneously, the sharp peaks at 1633, 1448, 1090 and 1329 cm -1 corresponding to the C=O, C=C, C-N and N-H vibrations, respectively. These results here demonstrate that e-PNPs bear abundant o-hydroxy, aminocarbonyl and carbonyl, enabling them to consist ESIPT units.

XPS spectra were examined to characterize the surface chemical composition of e-PNPs. As illustrated in Fig. 2 a, the XPS survey spectrum of the e-PNPs displayed three peaks at 286.3, 398.9, and 532.3 eV, corresponding to C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. In addition, the elemental analysis data of the e-PNPs indicate the following elemental compositions: carbon (79.25%), oxygen (16.05%), nitrogen (4.70%). In detail, the high resolution C1s XPS spectra in Fig. 2b shows four different states: O-C=O, C-O, C-C/C-N and C=C (286.4 eV, 286.1 eV, 284.3 eV and 284.1 eV). The high-resolution N1s spectra of the e-PNPs (Fig. 2c) indicate three peaks at 400.3, 400.1, and 399.5 eV, attributing to the formation of C-N-C, N-H and N-C, respectively.

The optical properties of the e-PNPs were investigated using UV-vis, fluorescence emission and fluorescence excitation spectroscopy measurements. The absorption spectrum of the e-PNPs in a PBS buffer (pH7.0) shows three clear absorption bands at 275 nm, 355 and 420 nm (Fig. 3 a). The absorption peak at 275 nm is linked to the π-π* transitions in the carbon core of the aromatic bonded groups. The prominent peak appeared at 355 nm is assigned to the n-π* transition of the C=O bonds. The absorption band at 420 nm may attribute to the tautomeric form of the ESIPT units in e-PNPs. Similarly, the peaks at 359 nm and 280 nm appeared in the fluorescence excitation spectrum of the e-PNPs (Fig. 3b) that can be attributed to the σ-π and π-π* transitions. When excited with a 360 nm UV lamp, two emission peaks at 425 and 520 nm were observed, which show typical ESIPT fluorescent properties (Fig. 3c). At the same time, the e-PNPs solution showed strong green luminescence (inset of Fig. 3d). Compared e-PNPs with simple ESIPT compound 2-hydoxy-N-phenylbenzamide, it was found that the e-FL of e-PNPs is stronger than that of 2-hydoxy-N-phenylbenzamide (Fig. S1). The absolute fluorescence quantum yield (FQ) was calculated to be 36.6% using the absolute method as previously reported, which is much higher than that of conventional small ESIPT dyes [33–35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing behavior of e-PNPs to nitrite
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explore the utilization of the newly developed ESIPT-fluorescent nanomaterials, we established a sensitive and selective assay and imaging of NO2 - using e-PNPs as probes. The principle of this signaling system based on the ESIPT emission quenching (e-FL) of e-PNPs is illustrated in Scheme 1b. The e-PNPs bear abundant o-hydroxyl and amide groups, which are responsible for the formation of ESIPT units. Attributing to the hydrophobic environment of nanostructures, the ESIPT units in e-PNPs can undergo an efficient ESIPT processes under the excitation of light, heat or magnet and display strong ESIPT fluorescence. Upon the addition of NO2 -, the reaction of amide with NO2 - can destroy the structure of ESIPT units, resulting in their e-FL quenching. These results are in accordance with the FL spectra, which clearly verified our proposed detection principle.

The sensing behavior of e-PNPs nanoprobes towards NO2 - was investigated in a PBS buffer (20 mM, pH7.0) at room temperature. As shown in Fig. 3a, when nitrite solution was introduced into the e-PNPs solution, the color changed from light yellow to brown red, accompanied by a definite altering of absorption spectrum. In order to get the best ESIPT emission, we optimized the exciting light by increasing excitation wavelengths from 320 nm on the shift of 20 nm increments. The fluorescence spectrum of e-PNPs indicate two emission peaks in PBS solution, representing a typical ESIPT emission. When excited with 420 nm light, strong ESIPT fluorescence of e-PNP can be obtained (Fig. 3c). Obviously, the addition of 10 μM NO2 - results in about 95% quenching of the ESIPT fluorescence intensity of e-PNPs. Meanwhile, an apparent change in the bright green fluorescence of e-PNPs solution under 365 nm UV light has nearly disappeared (inset of Fig. 3d).

In order to obtain better response of e-PNPs to NO2 -, it is necessary to optimize the conditions which can significantly influence the detection of NO2 -, such as the pH value, quenching time and reaction temperature. The pH has a strong impact on the ESIPT reaction of e-PNPs and the charge of NO2 -, therefore we have investigated the effect of pH value from 3.0 to 11.0 on the e-FL. As shown in Fig. S2a, the e-FL intensity of the e-PNPs showed only a slight decrease as the pH value vary from 4.5 to 9.0. When the pH value is less than 4.5, the e-FL of e-PNPs decreased significantly.

On the other hands, the higher pH will suppress the reactivity of NO2 -. When the pH value is higher than 9.0, nitrite will lose its reactivity. Therefore, we chose 7.0 as the pH value to test the response of the e-PNPs nanoprobe based on the comprehensive consideration.

At the relatively low concentrations, where the e-Fl measurements were performed, the reaction of e-PNPs with nitrite was found to be time- and temperature-dependent. Hence, the completion of the reaction was obtained by placing the colorimetric tube in a hot water bath. The effects of time and temperature on the reaction of e-PNPs with nitrite were presented in Fig. S2b. It was found that a higher temperature was necessary to get obvious e-FL quenching. But when the temperature was raised from 50 to 70 °C, the e-FL quenching was almost constant. At 50–70 °C, the e-FL quenching was basically stable after 10 min of reaction. Therefore, the optimized conditions for the analyzing process were found to be at 35 °C for 10 min.

To further examine the specificity of the e-PNPs for NO2 −, selectivity assays were carried out using potential interfering substances. As shown in Fig. 4 a, under identical conditions, in sharp contrast to NO2 −, other anion ions such as NO3 -, SO4 2- HSO4 -, F-, Cl-, Br-, CO3 2-, HCO3 -, AC -, P2O7 2-, H2PO4 -, ClO-, metal ions including Co2+, Na+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, as well as biologically related molecules (H2O2 and ATP) showed almost no influence on the spectra of the e-PNPs. Therefore, these results reveal that the e-PNPs are highly selective nanoprobes for NO2 − over the other tested interfering species. To evaluate anti-interference ability of the e-PNPs for NO2 −, we also tested several control experiments in the presence of potential interfering substances. The results suggest that negligible interference even if excesses the interfering metal ions coexisted, anion ions or biologically related molecules (Fig. 4b). Therefore, these results demonstrate that the e-PNPs strong anti-interference ability.

In order to demonstrate the analytical performance of the e-PNPs nanoprobes in complicated food samples, the NO2 - concentrations in food samples including smoked pork, sausage and pickled vegetables have been determined ﬂuorometrically using e-PNPs. Under the optimal conditions, the e-FL responses of e-PNPs to different concentrations of NO2 - were tested. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the e-FL of e-PNPs was quenched upon increasing NO2 - concentrations, and finally remained unchanged after the addition of more than 16 equiv. NO2 -. We deduce that the possible mechanism of the detection for NO2 - could be that NO2 - can react with the amide groups of e-PNPs and suppress their ESIPT process, resulting in the ESIPT fluorescence quenching; while the other interfering substances do not have such reactivity, so they do not bring effect on the e-FL of e-PNPs. Using e-PNPs as probes for NO2 - detection, a good linear correlation (R2 = 0.9987) between the quenching efficiency and the NO2 - concentration from 0 to 110 n M were obtained (Fig. 5b). The detection limit was calculated to be 2.3 nM (S/N = 3), which is far below the maximum contaminant NO2 - level in drinking water (1 ppm) [36]. Additionally, compared with some florescent methods and florescent probes [37,38], our strategy using e-PNPs for detecting nitrite is better than that of many related nitrite assays. The above analysis results demonstrating the potential of the e-PNPs could be used to fluorescent nanoprobes toward the determination of NO2 -.

Moreover, the sensing capability of the e-PNPs nanoprobes were examined by the quantitative detection of NO2 - in food samples. The proposed e-PNPs based method was applied to monitor NO2 - levels in smoked pork, sausage and pickle samples. The analytical results showed a good agreement with those obtained by standard methods based on GB 5009.33.2010 in literature [39]. The comparative analytical data and recovery values of various food samples are gathered Table 1 . The recoveries of the spiked samples ranged from 94.6% to 103.4%, indicating the feasible application ability of the proposed sensing system. These results suggest that our developed strategy has potential for monitoring NO2 - in food samples.

To evaluate the ability of e-PNPs for cell imaging, cellular uptake experiments in HeLa cells were then carried out and the fluorescence images were taken under a laser scanning confocal microscope. The results were given in Fig. 6 . Fig. 6a, b and c are the bright fields of Fig. 6d, e and f, respectively. The e-PNPs nanoprobes show no apparent toxicity to the HeLa cells even though the nanoprobe concentration was up to 100 μg/mL, thus the e-PNPs displayed low toxicity toward the cultured cell lines under the experimental conditions (Fig. 6d). In addition, it can be seen that the ingested e-PNPs mainly concentrated in the cytoplasm. These results also indicate that the e-PNPs can be rapidly delivered into the cytoplasm, implying the e-PNPs is good cell-permeable. Moreover, the e-PNPs exhibit highly fluorescent brightness and stability against photobleaching, which makes the probes great potential for imaging applications. Therefore, the ESIPT-exhibiting nanoprobe is also favourable for imaging of NO2 - in living cells owing to their good cell-permeability and biocompatibility.

Then, the practical application of the e-PNPs for monitoring intracellular NO2 - level in living cells was also examined. The HeLa cells were first incubated with the e-PNPs (50 μg/L) in a culture medium for 2 h and the ﬂuorescence images were then recorded using scanning confocal microscopy. A moderate emission wavelength ranging from 400 to 600 nm channel under excitation at 360 nm could be readily observed for HeLa cells incubated directly with the e-PNPs. To validate that the nanoprobe can respond to the changes of the NO2 - levels in living cells, we pretreated the HeLa cells with 1.5 and 10 μM/L NO2 -, respectively. As shown in Fig. 6e, the fluorescence is obviously weakened in the HeLa cells treated with 1.5 μM/L NO2 -, while those pretreated with 10 μM/L NO2 - showed that the fluorescence disappeared (Fig. 6f). Therefore, these results demonstrated the e-PNPs can be applied to tracking the intracellular NO2 - in living cells, suggesting our strategy has potential for clinical diagnosis.

In summary, we developed a facile and efficient approach to the synthesis of hompolymers by condensing 4-aminosalicylic acid and utilized the polymers to fabricate ESIPT-ﬂuorescent polymer nanoparticles (e-PNPs) by self-assembling in aqueous solution. The e-PNPs can exhibit enhanced ESIPT emission because of the hydrophobic micro-environment contributed by PNPs. Compared with conventional small ESIPT dyes, e-PNPs were endowed with strong ESIPT emission, bright luminescence, good water-solubility and excellent biocompatibility. The e-PNPs was used as specific nanoprobes to monitor NO2 - by e-FL quenching method. The e-PNPs based nanoprobe enables facile and robust assay of NO2 - in food samples with high sensitivity and selectivity, which allows for a rapid fluorimetric detection. The e-PNPs were also uploaded into living cells and employed for tracking intracellular NO2 - with high discrimination. Therefore the proposed e-PNPs nanoprobes provide a reliable method for the detection and imaging of NO2 - in food samples. This study may draw forth a new sight for the preparation of highly ESIPT-fluorescent nanomaterials and broadening application of them in fluorescence imaging.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no competing interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the Research Foundation of Education Bureau of Hunan Province (No. 18A141) and the authors also acknowledge the financial support from the Natural Science Foundation of State Key Laboratory of Life Organic Chemistry, Shanghai Institute of Organic Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences (No.SKLBNPC17344) and the Graduate Research and Innovation Foundation of Changsha University of Science and Technology (No.CX2019SS29).
